title: Cat Hair Loss Around Eyes: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat losing hair around their eyes? Learn about periocular alopecia, common causes, and why this condition usually has a low urgency level.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hair loss around a cat's eyes is usually a non-emergency condition caused by allergies or infections, but immediate veterinary care is needed if you notice squinting or eye discharge.

What does hair loss around my cat's eyes mean?

Hair loss around the eyes, medically referred to as periocular alopecia, is a common condition where the fur thins or disappears entirely in the area surrounding the eye sockets. This can be caused by various factors, including:

  • Self-trauma from scratching
  • Environmental allergies
  • Parasitic infections like mites
  • Fungal issues such as ringworm
  • Normal thinning of hair that occurs as cats age

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is losing hair around their eyes?

The urgency level for hair loss around the eyes is generally Low. While the underlying cause needs to be addressed by a veterinarian, it is rarely a life-threatening situation. However, you should schedule a vet visit more promptly if the hair loss is accompanied by:

  • Squinting
  • Yellow or green discharge
  • A change in the appearance of the eyeball itself
  • Risk of painful corneal ulcers or deep infections

How do photos of my cat's eyes help with veterinary triage?

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of your cat's eyes is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A photo allows a professional to:

  • See the condition of the skin underneath the fur (e.g., redness and crusting vs. clean, pink skin)
  • Distinguish between active infections and stress-related grooming or simple thinning
  • Determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen
  • Identify what diagnostic tests might be necessary

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acquired hair loss in cats can be focal, multifocal, symmetric, or generalized. Pruritus or pain is a common cause of acquired inflammatory alopecia, often stemming from infectious skin diseases, ectoparasites, or allergic skin diseases. Flea allergy dermatitis is a common cause of symmetric alopecia in cats, even without obvious flea infestation. Marked hair loss can also occur after respiratory infections. Consider underlying causes of pruritic disease when evaluating symmetric alopecia.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 842)

faqs.question: Could my cat have ringworm?
faqs.answer: Yes, ringworm is a common fungal infection that often causes circular patches of hair loss around the face and eyes. It is important to note that ringworm is zoonotic, meaning it can spread to humans and other pets.

faqs.question: Are certain breeds more prone to this?
faqs.answer: Yes, breeds with flatter faces (brachycephalic) like Persians or Himalayans may experience more skin fold irritation and moisture around the eyes, which can lead to secondary hair loss.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause hair loss near the eyes?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Food allergies or environmental triggers like dust and pollen can cause intense itching, leading a cat to rub their face against furniture or use their paws to scratch, resulting in fur loss.
